Sample Digest · Monday 27 April 2026

A recent Paediatrics digest,
as sent to subscribers

This is the real Monday digest from Monday 27 April 2026. Showing the first 8 of 32 articles from that week — every new paper from the top journals in Paediatrics.

32articles that week
4journals covered

Note: Summaries are AI-generated from publicly available PubMed abstracts. Always verify against the source article before clinical use.
JAMA pediatrics

First Antibiotic Prophylaxis Prescription Among Children With Sickle Cell Disease

Shi JS, LaFon E, Sutaria A et al. · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Insufficient information provided - abstract content not included.
Key Question: Cannot be determined from the title alone, though this appears to examine antibiotic prophylaxis prescribing patterns in paediatric sickle cell disease patients.
Key Findings:
  • No abstract provided - unable to determine specific findings
  • Study appears to focus on first antibiotic prophylaxis prescriptions in children with sickle cell disease
  • No data available on outcomes, prescribing patterns, or clinical results
Clinical Relevance: Antibiotic prophylaxis timing and prescribing practices are clinically important for UK paediatric sickle cell patients, as these children require pneumococcal prophylaxis from early infancy according to national guidelines.
Limitations: Cannot assess study limitations without access to the full abstract and methodology.
Note: This summary is incomplete as only the article title was provided. The abstract content would be needed to provide meaningful clinical insights about study design, sample size, outcomes, and clinical implications for UK paediatric practice.
PubMed →DOI →
JAMA pediatrics

Childhood Food Insecurity Beyond Traditional SNAP Income Thresholds

Gabbay JM, Muleta H, Castellanos MB et al. · 2026 Apr 20
PubMed →DOI →
JAMA pediatrics

Considering Youth Mental Health in Transgender Sports Policy

Taimish JY, Bailar S, Turban JL · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Commentary
Key Question: How should youth mental health considerations inform transgender sports policy decisions?
Key Findings:
  • Sports participation provides significant mental health benefits for transgender youth, who already face elevated rates of depression, anxiety, and suicidal ideation
  • Current policy debates often overlook the psychological impact of exclusion from sports on vulnerable young people
  • Mental health outcomes should be weighted alongside other considerations when developing inclusive sports policies
Clinical Relevance: UK paediatricians increasingly support transgender youth who may face sports exclusion policies that could exacerbate existing mental health disparities, making advocacy for evidence-based, youth-centred approaches clinically important.
Note: As a commentary piece, this provides expert perspective rather than empirical data on the intersection of sports policy and adolescent mental health in transgender youth.
PubMed →DOI →
JAMA pediatrics

Oral Vitamin K-US Newborns Need an FDA-Approved Option

Kair LR, Loyal J, Phillipi CA · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Commentary
Key Question: Should the US FDA approve an oral vitamin K formulation for newborn vitamin K deficiency bleeding (VKDB) prevention?
Key Findings:
  • Currently no FDA-approved oral vitamin K preparation exists for US newborns, despite availability in other countries
  • Some US parents refuse intramuscular vitamin K, leaving newborns at risk for potentially fatal VKDB
  • Oral formulations could provide an alternative for families declining injection while maintaining some protection
Clinical Relevance: While this addresses US regulatory gaps, UK clinicians should note that oral vitamin K options exist here but require multiple doses with uncertain compliance, reinforcing the importance of discussing both intramuscular and oral options with parents who decline standard prophylaxis.
Limitations: Commentary does not present new efficacy or safety data comparing oral versus intramuscular vitamin K formulations.
PubMed →DOI →
JAMA pediatrics

Cerebral Oximetry in Extremely Preterm Infants: 2-Year Follow-Up of the SafeBoosC-III Randomized Clinical Trial

Rasmussen MIS, Hansen ML, Pellicer A et al. · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: RCT follow-up study
Key Question: Does cerebral oximetry-guided treatment in the first 72 hours after birth improve 2-year neurodevelopmental outcomes in extremely preterm infants?
Key Findings:
  • Death or moderate/severe neurodevelopmental disability occurred in 47.1% (cerebral oximetry) vs 48.0% (usual care); RR 0.96 (97.5% CI 0.85-1.07, p=0.45)
  • Mean Bayley cognitive scores were nearly identical: 92.8 vs 93.2; mean difference -0.14 (97.5% CI -3.24 to 2.96, p=0.92)
  • No statistically significant differences between groups for either primary outcome
Clinical Relevance: This large multicentre trial provides definitive evidence that routine cerebral oximetry monitoring in extremely preterm infants does not improve long-term neurodevelopmental outcomes, informing UK neonatal unit protocols and resource allocation decisions.
Limitations: Follow-up was limited to 2 years corrected age, so longer-term educational and behavioural outcomes remain unknown.
PubMed →DOI →
JAMA pediatrics

Chronotherapeutic Approaches to Target Insufficient and Late Sleep in Adolescents: A Randomized Clinical Trial

Wescott DL, Quick AD, Oryshkewych NS et al. · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Randomized clinical trial
Key Question: Does a chronotherapeutic intervention combining sleep scheduling, morning bright-light glasses, and evening blue-light blocking glasses improve sleep duration and circadian timing in late-sleeping adolescents?
Key Findings:
  • Intervention group achieved 45 minutes earlier circadian timing (β = -0.55; 95% CI, -0.79 to -0.31; P = .003) and 47 minutes longer weeknight sleep duration (β = 0.74; 95% CI, 0.30-1.18; P = .003) compared to controls
  • The brief intervention (<1 hour initial session plus 2 weeks of light therapy and sleep scheduling) showed clinically meaningful improvements after just 2 weeks
  • Circadian alignment (DLMO-midsleep interval) improved but did not reach statistical significance
Clinical Relevance: This provides evidence for a practical, brief intervention that UK paediatric services could implement to address common adolescent sleep problems affecting academic performance and mental health.
Limitations: Short 2-week follow-up period limits understanding of longer-term effectiveness and sustainability of improvements.
PubMed →DOI →
JAMA pediatrics

NIH-Funded Pediatric Research

Forrest CB, Jeste SS, Shah SK · 2026 Apr 25
PubMed →DOI →
Pediatrics

Outcomes in Adolescents From Sequential vs Bilateral Cochlear Implantation in Young Children

Asp F, Eklöf M, Moumèn-Denanto F et al. · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Cross-sectional study
Key Question: Does simultaneous versus sequential bilateral cochlear implantation in early childhood affect long-term hearing outcomes in adolescents?
Key Findings:
  • Simultaneous implantation (n=17) achieved significantly better sound localisation than sequential implantation (n=20) with moderate effect size (Cohen's d=0.58, P<0.001)
  • Speech recognition in quiet and masking conditions was comparable between groups
  • Both groups performed worse than normal-hearing controls despite average 15 years of bilateral implant use
Clinical Relevance: This supports current UK practice favouring simultaneous bilateral cochlear implantation where feasible, as spatial hearing benefits persist into adolescence and may impact educational and social outcomes.
Limitations: Small sample size with only 34% of eligible participants included, limiting generalisability of findings.
PubMed →DOI →
